video_title: The government just sent a warning to stock influencers
transcript: Prominent short seller Andrew Left has been convicted of securities fraud. A federal jury found Left guilty of using social media to illegally influence stocks. Our Jake Connley has been following the case. I know Andrew Left. I've talked to Andrew Left before on the show. >> I believe that people should put their opinions online. If you believe in your opinion of the stock, put it online. >> He's been around in the media for a long time. um he's very active on X, you know, putting out his thesis on various things. And it's pretty common in the short selling community, I think, fair to say. You put out something, the stock reacts, maybe you cover your short or you sell, you close the position. >> You cover the position. >> Yes. >> So, the case is not necessarily just about his shortselling. Short sellers, right, >> have a very common model. Most of the time they'll spend time building up a short position in a stock. They'll publish a report saying X Y and Z is wrong with the stock, whether it's fraud or some uh problem with the company or something undisclosed. The idea being that once the report comes out and the market sees it, the stock drops and the short seller can profit from that trade. >> This case is much more about his social media postings. Basically, >> he went out on social media, made claims long or short on a stock, and then did something else or closed out the position really quickly. And prosecutor prosecutors were saying if you're going to say I'm short Roku in one example and then immediately close out a position, you should tell the people who are following you. Which would would kind of put a lot of investors who flip positions quickly and have social media followings >> kind of in a box, in a bind of when do what what do I owe the people who follow my takes right on X? Well, there was another case that the government brought up where um apparently he went on CNBC's Fast Money. He talked about a a particular company as a fraud and then he said he had covered only a small size of his position in the stock when apparently according to the government he had closed out the position entirely. So, you know, sort of like his what he says not matching what he does. But to your point, um this is not necessarily uncommon practice. There are other parts of the case. There are uh emails between him and other hedge funds basically saying, "Hey, you guys can profit off my trades and what I'm able to do on social media." >> Uh, you know, the quote from one of the emails, his quote hot voice with retail investors would mean the hedge funds he was working with could take candy from a baby, >> which is never going to play well in front of a jury, and also raises questions about ethics lines and uh disclosures and all of all sorts of things. the SEC is going to be really interested in. >> Yeah. Um, >> so I mean this guy is just interesting on the face of it too because if you recall he was well known for being a short seller during the sort of um height of the meme stock mania he was attacked right you know short sellers were sort of seen as villains and he said he was going to stop short selling >> he lost 20 million on a GameStop short and then said and then goes to X >> but then he came back right then he sort of came back in into the game eventually couldn't couldn't stay away >> that short position is also what kicked off the initial investigation to the left back in 2018. The FBI went and raided his house. And it seems they've been building a case for all the years leading up until 2024 when he was formerly indicted for uh several counts of securities fraud. >> And even this charge has not stopped him from posting. By the way, he posted um after this. He says, "Today I was found guilty." Um and of course he says he is not guilty of this effectively. He says, "I didn't you know, we disagree with the jury. This doesn't stop here. We'll keep fighting." >> He hints at an appeal. We'll we'll see what what exactly what kind of um form that takes. I mean, you and I were talking earlier this morning. There has been this ongoing debate about the value of short sellers in the market but traditionally they do sort of serve a purpose of trying to shine light because >> I I won't say for I don't know what the percentages of these of these um cases that turn out to be accurate but at least some number of them >> someone comes out and says this company is not reporting accurately or there's an outright fraud allegation and some of the time those allegations are borne out. >> Right. Right. And you've se Andrew Left is only famous because he got some calls right really early in his career. Uh China Everground Group back in 2012, Valiant Pharmaceuticals in 2015 disclosing real problems with the companies involved. Real problems with the stock. The stock fell. He made money on the short of course, but it did disclose something. And this is why the SEC has never regulated short sellers out of existence. Even though there is a part of the market that I think would say this is unethical. This goes against market guidelines. The idea here is if something is happening at a company, whether that be fraudulent or whether that be false reporting or anything else >> and the market doesn't know, the short seller is there to expose that information and then allow the light to shine. Give the market more information, make the market more fair is the argument. >> Yeah. However, where left, I think, has often been seen as deviating from a lot of short sellers is that when you think of the traditional short sellers who have done this for a long time, they operate really, really quietly until the day the report comes out and then they're super splashy. They do TV, they do the whole media circuit >> and then they're really quiet again. Ever since 2018, >> Left has done this very, very publicly and caught a lot of positive and negative attention for it. M. So the question is now what do other short sellers do as a result of this? We don't know the answer. >> And what do they owe their followers? Not even their investors who they may have a fiduciary duty to, but what do they owe their followers on X if they're going to post their take or if if I'm going to go post that I think >> Nvidia should be at a certain price target. What do I owe the people who follow me on X? Yeah, I don't know that the securities law is entirely clear on these points.
